title: Portable Property and the Freedom to Run: Why Seizure Resistance Matters

source.original: You Have 10 Years to Join the AI Economy | Michael Saylor

body_html:
Michael Saylor draws a sharp distinction between property you can move and property that's pinned to a jurisdiction. A building in Manhattan can be rezoned to agricultural land by a hostile regulator, its value destroyed overnight. A luxury yacht, by contrast, simply leaves the marina when a new tax is imposed — as famously happened on a Mediterranean island where the entire fleet vanished the day before a 2% luxury yacht tax took effect. The lesson is ancient and unambiguous: portable property survives; immovable property is eventually confiscated.

Real estate suffers from what Saylor calls the worst possible tax treatment: you're taxed when you buy it, taxed when you sell it, taxed when you inherit it, and taxed simply for holding it. There is no jurisdictional arbitrage available because buildings cannot run and cannot hide. This makes real estate the first target of every socialist, communist, or simply cash-strapped government in history. Rent control, zoning restrictions, and outright seizure are all tools governments deploy precisely because the property owner has no escape.

Gold fares somewhat better as a portable store of value, but its physical weight and visibility create critical vulnerabilities. Over any sufficiently long timeline, gold held in banks has been seized — in the United States, Japan, Germany, and virtually every nation on earth over the past century. Moving gold takes one to three years, and everyone knows where it's stored. The window between recognizing a threat and executing an escape is simply too narrow for physical metal.

Bitcoin fundamentally changes this equation. You can move billions of dollars of value across jurisdictions in minutes, not years. No customs agent can detect it. No bank needs to approve the transfer. No six-step authorization process is required. Saylor uses an evocative analogy: an armed society is a polite society. When a government knows it is impossible to confiscate your wealth, it is far less likely to try. Bitcoin makes the cost of theft so high that rational actors simply target easier prey.

This doesn't mean Bitcoin eliminates all risk — governments can still pass laws, and custodial arrangements carry counterparty exposure. But the optionality to change jurisdictions in an hour, to self-custody without any intermediary, and to route around hostile regulation creates a property right that no physical asset can match. Over a long enough timeline, Saylor argues, the mortality rate of every jurisdiction is 100%. The only defense is the ability to move.
